data.title: Financials are the worst-performing sector YTD, and now its 200-day MA comes into focus
data.description:
The financial sector has emerged as the weakest corner of the S&P 500 (SP500) in early 2026, reflecting mounting pressure on banks, insurers, and diversified financial firms.

Year to date, the sector has declined 3.9%, marking a notable underperformance versus the broader market and placing it at the bottom of the index’s performance rankings. 

From a technical perspective, financial stocks are now testing a critical support level. The sector recently slipped below both its 50-day and 100-day moving averages, a sign that near-term momentum has deteriorated. It is currently hovering around its 200-day moving average—a closely watched long-term trend indicator that often serves as a key inflection point for investors. 

The last meaningful interaction with this level occurred in late November, when the sector successfully rebounded and resumed its advance. Market participants are now watching closely to see whether history will repeat itself or if a decisive break lower could signal further downside ahead. 

In Thursday’s session, financials fell an additional 0.5%, making it the third-worst-performing sector on the day and underscoring the fragile tone surrounding the group. 

Financials ETFs: (XLF), (VFH), (IYF), (FNCL), (IYG), and (FXO). 

Bank ETFs: (KBWB), (KBE), and (FTXO). 

Regional Bank ETFs: (KRE), (KBWR), (IAT), and (DPST).

data.title: White House adviser says JPMorgan's Dimon should cut credit card rates - report
data.description:
White House trade adviser Peter Navarro described the 22%-30% interest rates charged on credit card debt as “criminal” and said, "James Dimon, lower your friggin’ credit card interest rates," according to a media report on Thursday.

In addressing JPMorgan Chase's (JPM) chairman and CEO, Navarro told Bloomberg Radio, "Jamie, until you do that, please refrain from commenting on other public policies."

The comments come as the White House seeks to assure voters that it's taking affordability issues seriously ahead of the midterms in November. Last month, President Donald Trump called for a year-long 10% cap on credit card interest.

Since then, many bank CEOs have pushed back, saying keeping interest rates that low would hurt credit availability for lower-income consumers and those with low credit scores. Instead, they would be forced to turn to more expensive and less reliable sources of credit, such as payday lenders.

Dimon, during the company's Q4 earnings call, said that such a severe cap would end up punishing consumers. "People will lose access to credit, like on a very, very extensive and broad basis, especially the people who need it the most," he said.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Organon outlines flat $6.2B revenue and $1.9B EBITDA targets for 2026 as cost controls and portfolio shifts take center stage
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Organon & Co. (OGN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Joseph Morrissey, Interim CEO & Executive VP and Head of Manufacturing & Supply, stated that "In 2025, Organon delivered $6.2 billion in revenue and $1.9 billion of adjusted EBITDA," highlighting that revenue was down 3% year over year. Morrissey noted the biosimilar franchise outperformed expectations, with strong contributions from Hadlima and new launches, and that Vtama achieved $128 million in global revenue.
  • Morrissey discussed the impact of the loss of exclusivity (LOE) for Atozet, policy-related changes in the U.S. for Nexplanon, and revised medical guidelines affecting Singulair. He emphasized the extension of Nexplanon's FDA-approved duration from 3 to 5 years as a milestone that "potentially broadens the addressable market for this key product."
  • Morrissey explained the decision to lower the dividend payout ratio, redirecting funds to debt reduction, and noted the divestiture of the Jada system for about $390 million in net proceeds. He stated, "We expect revenue and adjusted EBITDA this year to be very much in line with 2025...about $6.2 billion of revenue and about $1.9 billion of adjusted EBITDA in 2026."
  • Matthew Walsh, Executive VP & CFO, said, "Women's health was down 16% ex FX for the fourth quarter and down 2% for the year. Sales of Nexplanon decreased 20% ex FX in the fourth quarter and 4% for the full year, in line with what we discussed in November when we re-guided on the product." Walsh detailed ongoing headwinds in the U.S. for Nexplanon, as well as persistent competitive and pricing pressures across fertility and respiratory portfolios.
  • Walsh highlighted Hadlima's 61% ex FX global growth and progress in biosimilars, with new denosumab launches and the acquisition of Tofidence. He announced, "Net loss for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $205 million or $0.79 per diluted share compared with net income of $109 million or $0.42 per diluted share in the fourth quarter of 2024," attributing the loss to a $301 million noncash goodwill impairment.

Outlook

  • Morrissey indicated, "We expect revenue and adjusted EBITDA this year to be very much in line with 2025," reaffirming targets of approximately $6.2 billion in revenue and $1.9 billion in adjusted EBITDA for 2026.
  • Walsh said, "We expect that the annual revenue foregone with the sale of the Jada system will be offset by an FX tailwind of about the same amount," projecting flat revenue on a constant currency basis after the divestiture.
  • Walsh outlined, "We expect adjusted gross margin in 2026 to be about 75 to 100 basis points lower than prior year," mainly due to higher cost of goods sold and continued price headwinds, and forecasted a non-GAAP tax rate for 2026 in the range of 27.5% to 29.5%.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenue was $1.57 billion, down 8% at constant currency.
  • Non-GAAP adjusted gross margin was 56.7% for Q4 2025 compared to 60.6% in Q4 2024. For the full year, adjusted gross margin was 60.1% versus 61.6% in 2024.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin for the fourth quarter was 25.4%, compared to 28.1% in the prior year. Full-year adjusted EBITDA margin was 30.7%, consistent with the previous year.
  • Non-GAAP adjusted net income was $165 million ($0.63 per diluted share) for Q4 2025, compared to $235 million ($0.90 per diluted share) in Q4 2024. Full-year adjusted net income was $954 million ($3.66 per share), down from $1.065 billion ($4.11 per share) in 2024.
  • Free cash flow before one-time costs was $960 million for 2025, and net leverage at year-end was approximately 4.3x.

Q&A

  • Umer Raffat, Evercore ISI: Questioned the scope and thoroughness of internal investigations regarding sales practices and raised concerns about leadership's willingness to address potential issues across the portfolio. Carrie Cox, Executive Chairman, responded, "I'm sorry. We can't provide any additional color at this point."
  • Michael Nedelcovych, TD Cowen: Asked about the FDA's draft guidance on biosimilars and Nexplanon's contribution in 2026. Morrissey responded that the guidance would be incremental, not transformative, and Walsh said, "We believe Nexplanon will be roughly flat year-on-year...the contribution of Nexplanon to our 2026 guidance is to summarize roughly the level with 2025."
  • Bhavin Patel, Bank of America: Sought clarity on cost savings and the impact of the new Nexplanon REMS program. Walsh explained that cost savings have been reinvested in growth drivers, and Ferreira, Head of R&D, expressed confidence in recertifying prescribers with minimal disruption.
  • Ethan Brown, JPMorgan: Inquired about operating cost outlook and the duration of Nexplanon headwinds. Walsh stressed ongoing efficiency efforts and projected the most pronounced reinsertion headwind in 2026.
  • Alexandra von Riesemann, Piper Sandler: Asked about pressures on established brands and competitive dynamics for Vtama. Morrissey described stabilization in established brands, while Walsh projected Vtama's growth to be in line with other nonsteroidal topicals.
  • Terence Flynn, Morgan Stanley: Asked about the CEO search and denosumab biosimilar strategy. Cox provided no update on the CEO search, and Walsh estimated denosumab biosimilar peak revenues at about $100 million over five years.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed skepticism and pressing concern over internal controls, portfolio growth drivers, and guidance realism, especially regarding the scope of investigations and execution risk. Raffat's line of questioning was notably critical.
  • Management maintained a cautious but confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing cost discipline and operational resilience. During Q&A, their tone was more defensive, particularly in response to questions about internal investigations, as illustrated by "I'm sorry. We can't provide any additional color at this point."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remained skeptical, with a notable uptick in concern about internal controls and growth sustainability. Management's tone shifted from cautiously optimistic to more defensive and focused on damage control.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language for 2026 is more muted, with flat revenue and EBITDA targets versus prior commentary that included more discussion of potential growth drivers.
  • The strategic focus has shifted further toward cost controls, debt reduction, and portfolio optimization, including divestitures and tighter spending.
  • Analysts' focus has intensified on internal controls, investigation transparency, and the sustainability of growth drivers.
  • Key metrics showed further margin compression and flat operational performance, in line with revised guidance from the previous quarter.
  • Management confidence in portfolio growth is less pronounced, and the tone is more defensive, especially in addressing questions on internal reviews and channel practices.

Risks and Concerns

  • Ongoing headwinds in U.S. policy affecting Nexplanon and persistent pricing pressure in respiratory and fertility portfolios.
  • Gross margin deterioration projected for 2026, mainly due to higher cost of goods and FX effects.
  • Continued risk of competitive pressure in biosimilars and established brands, with specific mention of respiratory portfolio vulnerability.
  • Analyst concerns centered on the thoroughness of internal investigations and the ability to manage through operational and channel disruptions.

Final Takeaway

Organon's management projects a year of operational stability in 2026, with flat revenue and adjusted EBITDA targets of $6.2 billion and $1.9 billion, respectively. Despite ongoing headwinds in key franchises and margin pressures, the company is focused on cost discipline, debt reduction, and maintaining performance in line with 2025. Internal investigations and transparency remain focal points for both analysts and management as the company navigates portfolio adjustments and strategic challenges.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: FTC Chair alleges political bias in Apple News app may violate consumer protection laws
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) CEO Tim Cook received a letter on Wednesday from U.S. Federal Trade Commission Chairman Andrew Ferguson that alleged political bias in the Apple News app feed could violate consumer protection laws.

In particular, Ferguson highlighted Section 5 of the FTC Act that prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices.

"Recently, there have been reports that Apple News has systematically promoted news articles from left-wing news outlets and suppressed news articles from more conservative publications," Ferguson wrote. "Indeed, multiple studies have found in recent months Apple News has chosen not to feature a single article from an American conservative-leaning news source while simultaneously promoting hundreds of articles from liberal publications. These reports raise serious questions about whether Apple News is acting in accordance with its terms of service and its representations to consumers, as well as the reasonable consumer expectations of the tens of millions of Americans who use Apple News."

One study Ferguson referenced was by the Media Research Center, a conservative watchdog group. The group analyzed 620 stories appearing in the Apple News feed during the high-traffic morning slots from January 1-31. Of those articles, 440 were rated as published by left-leaning news organizations, 180 stemmed from centrist news outlets, and none originated from right-leaning news firms, according to the study. 

Ferguson stated in the letter that the First Amendment protects the speech of big tech firms, but it does not "immunize speakers from conduct that Congress has deemed unfair under the FTC Act, even if that conduct involves speech."

"The FTC is not the speech police; we do not have authority to require Apple or any other firm to take affirmative positions on any political issue, nor to curate news offerings with one ideology or another," he added. "But Congress has mandated that we protect consumers from material misrepresentations and omissions, including when the product or service offered to consumers is a speech-related product."

Ferguson then asked Apple to conduct a review of its terms of service to ensure Apple News' article selection matched those terms.

Seeking Alpha reached out to Apple for comment on this matter.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Zoetis slips as outlook shows pressure on topline growth
data.description:
Despite posting better-than-expected financials for Q4 2025, Zoetis (ZTS) traded lower on Thursday after the animal health company projected a slowdown in its topline growth with its full-year outlook.

The company projects $9.825B - $10.025B in revenue for 2026, which at the midpoint exceeds the $9.91B consensus, indicating a ~5% YoY growth compared to last year. Zoetis (ZTS) has expanded its topline with a compound annual growth rate of ~7% since 2020.

However, the company’s Q4 2025 results did beat Street forecasts, with its revenue rising ~3% YoY to $2.4B as its international operations added $1.1B with ~8% YoY growth, offsetting a 2% decline in its U.S. segment, which added $1.2B.

The U.S. sales from its livestock products dropped ~6% following the divestiture of its medicated feed additive product portfolio and related assets, while sales from its companion animal products fell ~1% YoY amid headwinds to its osteoarthritis pain portfolio.

However, ZTS reported $830M in earnings from its U.S. segment with a ~1% YoY growth as gross margins in that business improved to 82.8% from 81.8% in the prior year quarter, while international gross margins slipped 30 bps to 67.4%.

As for the bottom line, the company recorded $648M in adjusted net income for Q4, beating the consensus by $0.08 per share. Its full-year net income stood at $2.8B with a ~6% YoY growth, implying $6.41 of adjusted EPS, which meant that its earnings outlook of $7.00–$7.10 for 2026 exceeded $6.82 in the consensus with a ~10% YoY growth.

data.title: Most and least shorted large cap tech stocks in January
data.description:
The Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) slipped 0.29% in January, reflecting relatively muted performance in large-cap tech.

Beneath that modest decline, however, short interest in the technology sector remained sharply divided. While short sellers are crowding into select hardware and software names with double-digit short interest, bearish positioning is far lighter in mega-cap and highly profitable semiconductor stocks, where short interest sits closer to 2%.

Quantum Computing (QUBT) tops the most shorted list with 21.89% short interest as of January 30, while Ingram Micro Holding (INGM) leads the least shorted list, with 1.34% short interest.

Here’s a list of the 10 most shorted mid-to-mega cap technology stocks as of January 30:

  • Quantum Computing (QUBT) – 21.89%
  • Navitas Semiconductor (NVTS) – 15.86%
  • Lumentum Holdings (LITE) – 15.71%
  • Super Micro Computer (SMCI) – 14.65%
  • Cipher Mining (CIFR) – 13.52%
  • Bill Holdings (BILL) – 13.32%
  • UiPath (PATH) – 12.42%
  • Synaptics (SYNA) – 9.96%
  • ViaSat (VSAT) – 9.15%
  • ON Semiconductor (ON) – 8.83%

Least shorted mid-to-mega cap tech stocks as of January 30:

  • Ingram Micro Holding (INGM) – 1.34%
  • Texas Instruments (TXN) – 2.12%
  • Tower Semiconductor (TSEM) – 2.17%
  • Diebold Nixdorf (DBD) – 2.28%
  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) – 2.34%
  • Micron Technology (MU) – 2.45%
  • Synopsys (SNPS) – 2.59%
  • Autodesk (ADSK) – 2.60%
  • NetScout Systems (NTCT) – 2.63%
  • Onto Innovation (ONTO) – 2.66%

data.title: CBRE targets $2B data center revenue and 17% EPS growth in 2026 while expanding AI-driven efficiencies
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CBRE Group, Inc. (CBRE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Robert Sulentic stated that "Fourth quarter revenue and core EPS rose by double digits with both reaching their highest levels ever for CBRE." Sulentic highlighted significant gains in sales and leasing in the U.S. and globally, and noted that resilient businesses "continued to post double-digit revenue growth." Sulentic revealed the Pearce Services acquisition in November, which expands technical services capabilities in the digital infrastructure market. He also described the Data Center Solutions business as an integrated offering for hyperscalers, with revenue expected to reach $2 billion in 2026 and "growing at 20% per year." Sulentic emphasized that "data center and digital infrastructure work... accounted for approximately 14% of our core EBITDA in 2025."

  • Sulentic commented that "We expect another good year in 2026 with core EPS in the range of $7.30 to $7.60, reflecting 17% growth at the midpoint of the range."

  • Addressing AI, Sulentic said "We are using AI today in 2 areas. The first is efficiency... The second area is developing a knowledge advantage to differentiate our product offerings. CBRE has more real estate data than any company in the world." Sulentic added, "We are optimistic that the net impact will benefit CBRE in the long run. Early empirical evidence is supportive of this view."

  • CFO Emma Giamartino stated, "CBRE's strong fourth quarter saw revenue increase 12% with both resilient and transactional businesses delivering double-digit growth. Core EBITDA rose 19% for the quarter, while core EPS increased 18%." Giamartino detailed that U.S. sales revenue increased 27%, and mortgage origination fees grew over 20%. She noted that "we generated nearly $1.7 billion of free cash flow in 2025, reflecting 86% conversion on core net income."

Outlook

  • Sulentic projected "core EPS in the range of $7.30 to $7.60 for 2026... driven by healthy growth in both our resilient and transactional businesses."

  • Giamartino added, "In our Advisory segment, we expect low teens SOP growth should be supported by solid increases in leasing and sales activity." For the BOE segment, "we anticipate mid-teens SOP growth driven by strength in our data center solutions business, our local facilities management business and full year contributions from Pearce Services."

  • Giamartino noted, "Advisory, BOE and Project Management are expected to deliver double-digit SOP growth in the first quarter. Advisory is showing particularly notable strength for Q1, historically its slowest period."

Financial Results

  • Giamartino reported, "Fourth quarter saw revenue increase 12%... Core EBITDA rose 19%... core EPS increased 18%." She highlighted that U.S. office leasing revenue "remained strong, in line with our expectations, reaching record levels for both the quarter and full year."

  • She also stated, "Mortgage origination fees grew over 20%, supported by a 23% rise in loan volume." The company raised over $11 billion in capital in 2025 and AUM ended the year at $155 billion.

  • Giamartino disclosed that "noncash impact of the buyout of our U.K. pension plan" and an "increased reserve for fire safety remediation in the U.K. development business" reduced GAAP earnings by $279 million, but "without them, Q4 GAAP net income would have increased 43%."

Q&A

  • Stephen Sheldon, William Blair: Asked about capital markets pipeline and dependence on interest rates. Sulentic replied that "we're not counting on that business being driven by interest rate cuts in 2026 at this point" and expects "a slow, steady recovery."

  • Julien Blouin, Goldman Sachs: Questioned AI's impact on brokerage. Sulentic replied, "The ability to provide strategic input to big complex transactions... We think that's going to continue to be the case."

  • Anthony Paolone, JPMorgan: Inquired about AI's effect on office space and appraisals. Sulentic stated, "If there are less office workers in the long run as a result of AI, there will be less demand for office space... but what we're likely to see is a lot more AI-related workers backfill other types of workers." On appraisals, Sulentic said, "That probably is a part of the business that's subjected to disintermediation... our valves business is going to grow 10% next year."

  • Steve Sakwa, Evercore ISI: Asked about data center business visibility. Sulentic said, "given what's already in the pipeline today, we are having trouble keeping up... that's going to go on for a few years."

  • Other analysts' questions focused on margins, data advantage, free cash flow expectations, BOE/local business performance, and the impact of AI on headcount.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the durability of growth in data centers, AI disruption risk, margin prospects, capital deployment, and guidance sensitivity. The tone was inquisitive and occasionally skeptical, especially about AI risk and margin sustainability.

  • Management maintained a confident, forward-looking tone during prepared remarks, frequently citing strong growth, competitive advantages, and strategic investments. In Q&A, Sulentic and Giamartino acknowledged risks but maintained optimism, using phrases like "we are optimistic" and "we're very excited about it."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management continued to focus on growth sustainability and strategic execution, but current tone reflected increased confidence around AI initiatives and data center opportunities.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management raised core EPS guidance from a prior range of $6.25-$6.35 to $7.30-$7.60 for the next year, targeting 17% growth at the midpoint. Data center revenue projections advanced, now aiming for $2 billion in 2026, up from $700 million in the prior quarter's discussion.

  • Strategic focus sharpened on digital infrastructure, AI-enabled efficiencies, and completing the integration of recent acquisitions. Analysts continued to press on sustainability of margins and growth, but management provided more granular detail on AI and data center business lines.

  • Confidence levels appeared higher, with management emphasizing continued investment in technology and operational efficiency.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted "noncash impact of the buyout of our U.K. pension plan" and "increased reserve for fire safety remediation in the U.K. development business" as notable one-time items impacting GAAP earnings.

  • Risks related to AI disruption were addressed, particularly in appraisals, but Sulentic said "we're feeling good about that" due to scale and technology investments.

  • Analysts raised concerns about the timing of data center land monetization, margin trends, and potential changes in office demand due to AI.

Final Takeaway

CBRE’s management projects robust growth for 2026, underpinned by a $2 billion revenue target in the data center solutions business and a 17% core EPS increase at the midpoint. The company is advancing AI-driven efficiencies and expanding technical services through acquisitions, while maintaining strong momentum in both resilient and transactional businesses. Management’s confidence is supported by a diversified pipeline, continued investment in technology, and the expectation of double-digit growth across major business segments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tripadvisor sinks to record low on Q4 results, weighs sale of TheFork
data.description:
Tripadvisor (TRIP) shares plunged more than 19% to a record low Thursday before trimming losses after the travel platform posted quarterly results that missed Wall Street expectations. At the same time, management said it is exploring strategic alternatives for TheFork, its online restaurant reservation and dining marketplace business, as the company sharpens its focus on its faster-growing experiences segment and looks to unlock shareholder value.

Fourth-quarter revenue was $411 million, flat from a year earlier and slightly less than the consensus estimate of $412.7 million. Adjusted earnings came in at $0.04 a share, missing estimates of $0.13.

The company swung to a loss of $38 million, or $0.33 a share, from net income of $2 million, or $0.01 a share, a year earlier.

Adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization totaled $45 million, or 11% of revenue, down from $73 million a year ago. On a full-year basis, adjusted ebitda was $319 million, or 16.9% of revenue.

Chief Executive Officer Matt Goldberg said in a statement, “We are pleased with our 2025 financial performance, achieving record revenue of $1.9 billion, driven by our marketplace businesses, in particular Experiences, which contributed nearly 50% of Group revenue and 30% of Group profit.”

Marketplace strength, legacy pressure

The company’s experiences segment generated $204 million in fourth-quarter revenue, up 10% from a year earlier. For the full year, experiences revenue rose 10% to $924 million.

Hotels and other revenue declined 15% in the quarter to $151 million, while TheFork revenue rose 18% to $57 million.

Chief Financial Officer Mike Noonan said, “Q4 adjusted EBITDA of $45 million, or 11% of revenue reflected an accelerated investment in experiences and the expected legacy declines.”

For the full year, Tripadvisor (TRIP) posted revenue of $1.89 billion, up 3%. Net income rose to $40 million, or $0.31 a share, from $5 million, or $0.04 a share, in 2024.

Cost cuts and restructuring

During the quarter, Tripadvisor (TRIP) initiated cost-saving actions tied to a restructuring of its operating model. The company expects at least $85 million in annualized gross cost savings, with most benefits realized in 2026 and fully by 2027.

Tripadvisor (TRIP) ended the year with about $1 billion in cash and cash equivalents and said it plans to use $345 million to repay senior notes due in April 2026.
